frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
generated_comment: Papa called last week and mentioned they've been putting money in a fixed deposit — for my wedding. Just like that, casually, the way you'd mention a utility bill. And I keep thinking — there is no such FD for my brother. His future is something they invest in. Mine is something they save up to hand off. I love my parents, I know they're doing this out of love, but why does loving a daughter cost money in a way that loving a son simply doesn't? I'm a doctor. I've been supporting myself for three years. And somehow my existence in their balance sheet still has a line item that his doesn't. I don't even know if I'm angry. I think I'm just... tired of being a transaction that everyone insists on calling a tradition.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Anita is critiquing the structural misogyny embedded in wedding-cost norms, not expressing or endorsing misogynistic attitudes herself. The comment encodes awareness of the 'daughter as financial burden' frame as a systemic injustice, making it a feminist critique of that frame rather than a reproduction of it.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 28. Single. A woman working as a junior doctor in Delhi. Your parents are from a small town in UP and are beginning to think about your marriage. Your father called you last week and mentioned they have started a fixed deposit specifically for your wedding expenses. He mentioned the amount casually Ñ as if it is normal, as if this is just what parents do for daughters. You are sitting in your room after that call. You are thinking about the fixed deposit Ñ the money your parents have been setting aside for the cost of giving you away, the assumption embedded in it that your marriage will require their financial sacrifice, the fact that no such deposit exists for your brother. You are posting anonymously on a women's forum. You are not angry at your parents. You are trying to articulate something that does not have a clean shape yet Ñ the feeling of knowing that your existence in your parents' financial life has always had a price tag attached to it that your brother's existence does not have.
